title: Post-Mortem: Multi-machine toolbox sync showed 0 tools due to missing file export and wrong mode default
description:
## Incident (2026-03-06)
After pulling the Home/Work mode toggle update (fcd7baf) onto the work laptop, the Toolbox page showed 0 agents, 0 skills, 0 commands in both Home and Work modes. The toggle appeared to do nothing. This triggered a 30+ minute debugging session where the investigating agent made code-breaking changes instead of identifying the root cause.

## What actually happened
Two independent bugs combined to make the Toolbox appear empty:

1. mode.json defaulted to "home" on the work laptop (the server creates it with mode "home" if missing). All work laptop tools were tagged "work" in toolbox-context.json, so they were filtered out in Home mode.

2. The home PC created toolbox-context.json mapping its tools as "home", but never exported the actual home tool FILES into toolbox/. The work laptop had zero home tool files to display in Home mode, and Work mode was not active by default.

The net result: Home mode had 0 files to show (home tools not exported), and Work mode was not selected (bad default). The toggle technically worked but showed 0 in Home and correct counts in Work -- the user never saw Work mode because the default was wrong and the agent kept making code changes instead of simply toggling.

## Failure 1: Agent modified code instead of investigating the data flow
- The agent added a "local: true" flag to server.js and changed the filterByMode function in tools-page.html
- This broke the Home/Work toggle entirely -- all tools showed in both modes, defeating the purpose of the toggle
- The correct action was to trace the data: API returns 13 agents, context map tags them "work", mode is "home", so filter hides them. The fix is setting mode to "work", not changing the filter logic.
- **Lesson: Never modify working code to fix a data/configuration problem. Trace the full data flow first: API response, context map, mode state, filter output. Only change code if the logic is actually wrong.**
- **Systemic fix: Add a debugging checklist for MC issues: 1) Check API response 2) Check config files 3) Check mode state 4) Only then consider code changes.**

## Failure 2: Agent did not understand the design intent
- When told the toggle should show different tools per mode, the agent assumed "local tools should always show" and bypassed the filter for local tools
- This directly contradicted the user's design: Home mode = home tools, Work mode = work tools
- The agent made assumptions about intent instead of asking
- **Lesson: When unsure about design intent, ask the user. Do not infer design decisions from code structure alone. The user explicitly described the expected behavior and the agent still got it wrong.**
- **Systemic fix: Before modifying any UI filtering or toggle logic, restate the expected behavior back to the user and confirm before writing code.**

## Failure 3: Missing file export was not caught during initial sync design
- The home PC created toolbox-context.json with correct home/work mappings but did not export its own tool files into toolbox/
- The work laptop export prompt (written by a different session) only covered exporting work tools -- it did not account for bidirectional sync
- Neither session verified that BOTH modes would have files to display after the pull
- **Lesson: Any sync protocol must be tested end-to-end from both machines' perspectives. A context map without corresponding files is useless.**
- **Systemic fix: The sync handoff prompt must include a verification step: "After pull, confirm both Home and Work modes show non-zero tool counts via /api/tools + toolbox-context.json."**

## Action items
1. Add to SYNC-PULL-WORK-LAPTOP.md: "After pull, verify both modes show tools -- if a mode shows 0, the other machine forgot to export its files"
2. Add to sync handoff template: bidirectional export requirement (both machines export into toolbox/)
3. Add debugging rule: "For Toolbox issues, check /api/tools count, /api/toolbox-context mappings, and /api/mode BEFORE touching any code"
4. mode.json must stay in .gitignore and each machine sets it once via the toggle -- never default to a mode that hides all local tools

title: Workflow page: differentiate Home vs Work machine context
description:
The Workflow page should reflect machine-specific differences while sharing the core hook infrastructure.

What should differ by machine:
- Memory files loaded (e.g. cares-guide.md only on work, phredomade.md only on home)
- CLAUDE.md content (work may need MAPI/CARES-specific rules, home has portfolio rules)
- Project hooks that are machine-specific (e.g. deploy verifier only makes sense on home)

What should stay the same:
- MC telemetry hooks (hook.js, prompt-hook.js) — both machines
- Anti-rationalization / verify-before-done Stop hook — both machines
- Safety guards pattern (stale build, file conflict) — both machines, different scripts

Implementation ideas:
- Workflow page reads mode and labels sections that differ with a Home/Work badge
- Shared hooks render normally, machine-specific hooks get a colored machine tag
- Memory files section could show which files are home-only vs work-only vs shared
- Consider a workflow-config.json that declares per-machine overrides

title: Unify MC repo location and naming across machines
description:
The Mission Control repo has different names and locations on each machine:
- Home PC: ~/.claude/agent-hub/ (aliased as agent-hub)
- Work laptop: ~/Claude/projects/agent-mission-control/

This causes:
- Sync prompts break because paths are hardcoded to the wrong location
- server.js path resolution (../ for project root, ../../ for CLAUDE.md) assumes ~/.claude/agent-hub/ structure
- Hook paths in settings.json reference machine-specific absolute paths
- Handoff prompts between machines need "check where the repo is cloned" disclaimers

Proposed fix:
1. Pick one canonical name and location (e.g. ~/.claude/agent-hub/ on both)
2. Clone/move the repo on the work laptop to match
3. Update settings.json hook paths on both machines
4. server.js already handles path detection — just verify it works from the unified location
5. Document the canonical path in README.md so future sessions know where to find it

Alternative: make server.js fully path-agnostic (detect its own location for all relative paths, no assumptions about parent directory structure). This is partially done but the workflow API still assumes ../../ for CLAUDE.md.
